vulnerability.title: Restrict Exposed Kernel Pointer Addresses Access
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.kptr_restrict kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict= To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.kptr_restrict =
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.kptr_restrict from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.kptr_restrict.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.kptr_restrict" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl config file which to sa

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only adjusts a kernel sysctl parameter to restrict pointer exposure. This change does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem layout, and it can be applied at runtime without requiring a reboot. The risk of breaking critical services is minimal; the only possible impact is on debugging tools that rely on kernel pointer visibility.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: Potential incompatibility with debugging or profiling tools that expect unrestricted kernel pointers; Misconfiguration could leave the parameter unset if edit syntax is incorrect

execution_details.stdout:
# when using 1 interface for two networks when using NETKEY, the kernel
# thinks it can be clever by sending a redirect (cause it cannot tell
# an encrypted packet came in, but a decrypted packet came out),
# so it sends a bogus ICMP redirect
#
# We disable redirects for XFRM/IPsec
# net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
# net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
# net.ipv4.conf.default.send_redirects = 0
# net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
# net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0
# net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0

# sysctl settings are defined through files in
# /usr/lib/sysctl.d/, /run/sysctl.d/, and /etc/sysctl.d/.
#
# Vendors settings live in /usr/lib/sysctl.d/.
# To override a whole file, create a new file with the same in
# /etc/sysctl.d/ and put new settings there. To override
# only specific settings, add a file with a lexically later
# name in /etc/sysctl.d/ and put new settings there.
#
# For more information, see sysctl.conf(5) and sysctl.d(5).
# Per : Set net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_ra = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_ra = 0

llm_verdict.message: Happy to help! If you have any other OpenSCAP findings, configuration tweaks, or general questions about Rocky Linux 10, just let me know and I’ll take care of them.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly sets kernel.kptr_restrict to 2, makes the change persistent, and applies it at runtime. It will remediate the vulnerability.
concerns: Commenting out existing lines is unnecessary complexity and may miss non-.conf files that still set the parameter.
Using sed -i without --follow-symlinks could break symlinked config files.
The chosen filename 60-kernel-kptr-restrict.conf could be overridden by later sysctl.d files with a higher lexical order.
No backup of original config files before modification.
suggested_improvements: Create the persistent file with a higher priority number (e.g., 99-kernel-kptr-restrict.conf) to ensure it overrides earlier settings.
Use sed --follow-symlinks when editing symlinked files or avoid editing symlinks altogether.
Consider backing up files before in-place edits (e.g., cp file file.bak).
Simplify by creating the new config file without commenting out existing entries, as later files take precedence.
